# 50 Deep Questions for Opus — AAIML 2027 Paper Strategy

**Date:** 2026-07-06
**Purpose:** Master question list to fuel paper writing decisions, ensure claims are defensible, and surface every weakness an AAIML reviewer could attack. Each question includes: (a) the question, (b) why it matters for the paper, (c) evidence we have, (d) evidence missing.

**Provenance:** Generated by 10 specialized agents (Detection, PSR, Activity, Head Pose, Architecture, Training Infra, Eval Pipeline, SOTA Comparison, Paper Writing, Adversarial Reviewer).

**Total:** 66 deep questions (target was 50; agents produced extras).

---

## Section 1: Detection (ASD) — Deep Questions

### D-1: What is the true detection ground truth when our evaluation pipeline gives NaN on the full validation set?

(a) D3 full-eval runs return det_mAP50=NaN on 38,036 frames while the subsample returns 0.358. Why?
(b) A NaN number in the main result table would invite an immediate rejection. We must either fix the bug or document it transparently.
(c) `src/runs/rf_stages/checkpoints/d3_v4/metrics.json` (NaN), `src/runs/rf_stages/checkpoints/d3_full_eval/metrics.json` (also NaN).
(d) subprocess_eval.py data-loading root cause; 10-seed variance on full eval.

### D-2: Should the paper claim "detection mAP=0.995, beats SOTA" from the separately-trained YOLOv8m, or is this a category error?

(a) Two detection stories conflict: our main ConvNeXt-Tiny achieves 0.358 mAP, but a separately-trained YOLOv8m (d1r) achieves 0.995 — beating WACV 2024's 0.838. Are both stories part of one paper?
(b) AAIML reviewers will spot the contradiction. "BEATS SOTA" claim from a different model is misleading.
(c) `src/runs/rf_stages/checkpoints/d1_yolov8m/metrics.json` (0.0004 main), `runs/detect/src/runs/yolov8m_industreal/d1r/results.csv` (0.995 separate).
(d) Whether the YOLOv8m was trained on the same data split; whether 0.358 is on the same val split as 0.995.

### D-3: What is the correct IoU threshold for comparing our detection metrics to WACV 2024 SOTA?

(a) WACV's reported 0.838 may be mAP@0.5:0.95 (COCO protocol) while our 0.358 is mAP@0.5 (PASCAL protocol). The 2.3x gap may be a measurement artifact.
(b) Mixing protocols is a known reviewer attack vector.
(c) `analyses/consult_2026_06_10/AAIML/industreal-all-papers-benchmarks.md` for SOTA numbers, `src/runs/rf_stages/checkpoints/d1_yolov8m/metrics.json` for our protocol.
(d) The exact WACV IoU protocol — paper section re-read needed.

### D-4: How severely does class imbalance affect our detection metrics, and should we report class-balanced or macro-averaged mAP?

(a) Six of 24 classes have zero GT instances in our val set. Standard mAP=0.358 vs present-class mAP=0.573 — a 0.215 zero-GT penalty.
(b) Reviewers will ask whether mAP=0.358 is inflated by easy "background" classes. Present-class mAP may be more honest.
(c) `src/runs/rf_stages/checkpoints/d1_yolov8m/metrics.json` det_per_class_gt distribution shows zeros.
(d) Whether WACV/SOTA used standard or present-class mAP — protocol verification.

### D-5: Can we claim "detection efficiency" when our mAP is 0.358 vs SOTA 0.838, but a free ultralytics YOLOv8m on the same data achieves 0.995?

(a) The efficiency thesis collapses if anyone can run `yolov8m.pt` for 1 day and beat both us and SOTA. The paper's value proposition must address this directly.
(b) AAIML reviewers will run the same baseline themselves.
(c) `runs/detect/src/runs/yolov8m_industreal/d1r/results.csv` shows 0.995 from ultralytics pipeline.
(d) Compute cost comparison: training time, parameter count, FPS.

### D-6: Should we use the self-trained YOLOv8m (mAP=0.995) as a teacher for knowledge distillation?

(a) Distilling the 0.995 YOLOv8m into the ConvNeXt-Tiny could flip detection from weakness to methodology contribution.
(b) Reviewers will accept "we propose a knowledge-distillation path" as a forward-looking contribution.
(c) `runs/detect/src/runs/yolov8m_industreal/d1r/weights/best.pt` available.
(d) Whether the paper has the page budget for a distillation experiment.

### D-7: Is "error state" detection (class 23) a separate evaluation problem requiring different metrics than mAP?

(a) WACV reports error-state FPR=65%. Our val set has 0 GT for error class — we can't evaluate it with mAP.
(b) Reporting error-state FPR/FNR vs mAP could distinguish our contribution from WACV's.
(c) `src/runs/rf_stages/checkpoints/d1_yolov8m/metrics.json` shows error_state gt=0.
(d) Whether the dataset's "error_state" GT is sparse by design or a labeling gap.

---

## Section 2: PSR (Procedure Step Recognition) — Deep Questions

### PSR-1: The POS Paradox — Why Does POS=0.968 Not Translate to Good F1?

(a) Our POS=0.968 is higher than SOTA's 0.812, but F1=0.7499 < STORM's 0.901. The reviewer-3 file flags POS as a metric artifact from MonotonicDecoder's fill-forward ordering constraint matching the GT ordering.
(b) Reporting high POS without context is misleading. Must explain the paradox or drop POS from headline metrics.
(c) `src/runs/rf_stages/checkpoints/SOTA_STATUS.md` (POS=0.968), `analyses/consult_2026_06_10/AAIML/reviewer-3-psr-paradigm-reconciliation.md`.
(d) Null model baseline: what POS would a "predict-all-zeros" decoder achieve? If ≥ 0.95, our POS is meaningless.

### PSR-2: Transition-Based F1 vs Per-Frame F1 — Which Metric Reflects Real Utility?

(a) B3/STORM use transition F1 (event matching within tolerance). Our 0.7499 is per-frame F1. Are these comparable?
(b) Different metrics measure different things. Mixing them in a single table is a reviewer attack.
(c) `src/evaluation/psr_optimal_thresholds.py` uses per-frame F1; `eval_yolov8m_psr.py:308` uses event F1.
(d) A side-by-side run with both metrics on the same predictions.

### PSR-3: Kendall Suppression — Why Does Kendall Weighting Kill the PSR Head?

(a) log_var_psr=-0.04 means the Kendall weight gives PSR precision ~1.04. Yet PSR receives near-zero gradients (liveness=DEAD, 569 steps of psr=0.0000 in train.log).
(b) The paper's claim that Kendall is "automatic multi-task balancing" contradicts the empirical PSR collapse.
(c) `src/runs/full_multi_task_tma_tbank_benchmark/logs/train.log`, `/tmp/train_ep24_smaller.log` KENDALL outputs.
(d) A controlled ablation with fixed weights (KENDALL_FIXED_WEIGHTS=True).

### PSR-4: Is D4 F1=0 a Genuine Failure or a Sparse-Signal Metric Collapse?

(a) D4 (YOLOv8m → MonotonicDecoder) yields F1=0.000 with POS=0.999 and Edit=0.994. If PSR's POS is 0.999 on YOLOv8m input, is the PSR head actually doing anything?
(b) D4 is the reviewer-3 backbone-swap experiment; F1=0 means our decoder is either redundant or broken.
(c) `src/runs/rf_stages/checkpoints/d4_yolov8m_psr/metrics.json` (F1=0, POS=0.999).
(d) A controlled comparison with a "predict all zeros" baseline — would it also get POS=0.999?

### PSR-5: Optimal Threshold Variation — Paper Claim or Data Snooping Artifact?

(a) psr_optimal_thresholds.py sweeps 19 thresholds × 11 components on the validation set, reporting optimal macro-F1=0.7499. With 209 effective combinations, val overfitting is plausible.
(b) Reviewers will demand a held-out test split.
(c) `src/runs/rf_stages/checkpoints/psr_optimal_thr/optimal_thresholds.json`.
(d) Leave-one-recording-out cross-validation of the optimal thresholds.

### PSR-6: The Dead Head Pattern — Are All 11 PSR Sub-Heads Indistinguishable?

(a) liveness reports psr=1.00e-06 DEAD across all components. If all sub-heads are dead, F1=0.7499 is purely backbone contribution.
(b) Reviewers will ask: what did your PSR head actually learn?
(c) `/tmp/train_ep24_smaller.log` LIVENESS lines.
(d) Per-component gradient magnitude, per-component prediction entropy.

### PSR-7: Sequence-Mode vs Per-Frame — Which Training Path Actually Updates PSR?

(a) Sequence-mode batches (T=8) occasionally show psr=1.5–4.5; per-frame batches show psr=0.0000. Only sequence batches update the head, and they're 25% of all batches.
(b) The PSR head is learning only from a quarter of the data — explaining the limited F1.
(c) `src/config.py` PSR_SEQ_EVERY_N_BATCHES=4; train.log sequence=1 markers.
(d) Whether per-component F1 is higher for components that appear in sequence batches.

---

## Section 3: Activity (Per-Frame Action Classification) — Deep Questions

### ACT-1: Why does our per-frame MLP achieve 0.028 clip-level top1 when the T3 MViTv2-S baseline gets 0.622?

(a) 22x gap. Either the MLP architecture is fundamentally inadequate (no temporal reasoning), or our training has a pathology (class-0 collapse).
(b) AAIML reviewers will ask if the paper has a competent activity method or just a probe.
(c) `src/runs/rf_stages/checkpoints/activity_clip_ep18/activity_clip.json` (0.028), `src/runs/rf_stages/checkpoints/t3_full_eval.json` (0.622).
(d) Whether the activity head's class-0 collapse persists in longer training.

### ACT-2: Is a per-frame MLP defensible as the paper's activity method, or does the paper need MViTv2-S or VideoMAE?

(a) The simple per-frame MLP cannot distinguish actions like take/put/fit on the same object (temporally identical). MViTv2-S has 8.2M parameters with TCN+ViT for temporal context.
(b) AAIML reviewers will compare us to any published action-classification baseline.
(c) `src/models/model.py` ActivityHead (150K params), `analyses/consult_2026_06_10/AAIML/13_ARCHITECTURE_REWRITE.md`.
(d) Inference-time latency comparison.

### ACT-3: Verb grouping (75 to 69 classes, hybrid mode) or full 75-class taxonomy?

(a) Hybrid grouping maps 75 raw action IDs to 69 verb groups. Comparability with T3's 0.622 depends on whether T3 also uses grouping.
(b) If T3 uses raw 75 classes, our 0.622 is on a different label space than our 0.028.
(c) `src/runs/rf_stages/checkpoints/act_remap_75_to_69.json`, `src/config.py` ACT_CLASS_GROUPING='hybrid'.
(d) T3's exact class protocol (raw vs grouped).

### ACT-4: Is the per-frame MLP fundamentally incapable of distinguishing actions that differ only in temporal structure?

(a) Actions like "take" vs "put" of the same object are visually identical in a single frame. The MLP can only see one frame.
(b) The paper needs an architectural justification or a temporal-context feature injection.
(c) `src/models/model.py` ActivityHead structure.
(d) Whether backbone features (ConvNeXt-Tiny) implicitly encode temporal context.

### ACT-5: What is the correct evaluation protocol — clip-level top1, per-frame accuracy, or macro-F1?

(a) Per-frame top1=0.023, clip-level top1=0.028, no macro-F1 reported. Reviewers will compare protocols.
(b) Different papers use different protocols; we should pick one and justify.
(c) `src/runs/rf_stages/checkpoints/SOTA_STATUS.md` reports both.
(d) Which protocol T3/B1/B2 use.

### ACT-6: Should the non-simple ActivityHead (TCN + 2 ViT blocks, T=16) be trained as an ablation?

(a) The codebase has a 8.2M-param TCN+ViT head disabled by ACTIVITY_HEAD_SIMPLE=True. Training it as an ablation isolates architectural vs training-data issues.
(b) Reviewers will accept architectural ablation.
(c) `src/config.py` ACTIVITY_HEAD_SIMPLE=True.
(d) Compute budget for a TCN+ViT training run (~2 days per ablation).

### ACT-7: What is the actual per-frame action classification SOTA on IndustReal, and does our MLP establish a legitimate first baseline?

(a) The 0.023 per-frame top1 may be the first published per-frame baseline on IndustReal — a defensible contribution.
(b) "First baseline" is a weaker claim than "SOTA" but still publishable.
(c) `analyses/consult_2026_06_10/AAIML/industreal-sota-benchmarks.md` (no per-frame baseline listed).
(d) A literature survey for per-frame action classification on IndustReal.

---

## Section 4: Head Pose — Deep Questions

### HP-1: Forward MAE vs SOTA — Is the "~15°" Reference Real?

(a) SOTA_STATUS claims "near SOTA ~15°" but the 15° number is unsourced. Either our ~8° beats a real baseline (needs citation), or there is no prior IndustReal ego-pose baseline.
(b) A reviewer attack: "what SOTA? Cite or retract the claim."
(c) `src/runs/rf_stages/checkpoints/SOTA_STATUS.md`.
(d) A literature search for ego-pose (not face-pose) on IndustReal.

### HP-2: Up-Vector Reliability — Three Conflicting Numbers

(a) Three up-vector numbers exist: 7.06° (day-1-checkpoint), 13.5° (300-subset), 26.20° (full eval). The 4x gap cannot go unexplained.
(b) Reporting all three invites reviewer confusion. Pick one and document why.
(c) `analyses/consult_2026_06_10/AAIML/day1-checkpoint-done-and-next-steps.md`, `SOTA_STATUS.md`.
(d) Per-recording breakdown, error distribution analysis.

### HP-3: Position Units Controversy — "DO NOT USE FOR REPORTING"

(a) The eval code explicitly says "DO NOT USE FOR REPORTING" on position (mm/cm). The unit ambiguity (HEAD_POSE_POS_SCALE=100 → meters→cm? or loss-weighting?) must be resolved.
(b) Reporting position without resolution is a self-inflicted reviewer attack.
(c) `src/evaluation/evaluate.py:1918-1926` "DO NOT USE FOR REPORTING".
(d) Confirmation from HoloLens SDK or IndustReal authors on position units.

### HP-4: Category Error with Face-Based Estimators (OpenFace, 6DRepNet)

(a) OpenFace/6DRepNet are face-based pose estimators. Comparing ego-pose to face-pose is a category error per reviewer-4. But silence invites reader comparison.
(b) Proactive taxonomy distinction or survey of legitimate ego-pose literature.
(c) `analyses/consult_2026_06_10/AAIML/reviewer-4-ego-pose-contribution.md`.
(d) Survey of egocentric pose literature (HoloLens, IMU-based).

### HP-5: FiLM Conditioning Contribution — Unmeasured Ablation

(a) `use_headpose_film=True` but no ablation isolates FiLM's effect. The ablation suite does not include FiLM-on vs FiLM-off for the pose head.
(b) Reviewers will ask: "Did FiLM contribute? Show the ablation."
(c) `src/models/model.py` FiLM layers, `src/config.py`.
(d) A 2-day ablation run.

### HP-6: Single-Frame vs Temporal Headroom

(a) The paper claims single-frame inference as a strength. Temporal smoothing (moving average, Kalman filter) would likely improve results. The potential gain is unmeasured.
(b) Reviewers will compare against temporally-smoothed baselines.
(c) `analyses/consult_2026_06_10/AAIML/04_BEST_PAPER_FORMULA.md`.
(d) A simple temporal-filtering post-processing experiment.

---

## Section 5: Architecture — Deep Questions

### A-1: What does Kendall uncertainty weighting actually buy when one head (PSR) receives near-zero gradient and the weighting requires manual overrides?

(a) Kendall's log_var_psr=-0.04 gives PSR precision 1.04. The weighting is supposed to auto-balance, but PSR is suppressed by KENDALL_HP_PREC_CAP, KENDALL_FIXED_WEIGHTS bypass, and DETACH_PSR_FPN isolation.
(b) Reviewers will ask whether Kendall is principled or just a knob.
(c) `src/training/train.py`, `src/config.py` Kendall settings, train.log.
(d) Fixed-weight ablation (KENDALL_FIXED_WEIGHTS=True).

### A-2: Is the two-stage FiLM conditioning actually modulating backbone features, or is it processing noise from pseudo-keypoints?

(a) FiLM layers use hand and headpose pseudo-keypoints. The detach at lines 696, 2044, 2168 means pseudo-keypoints don't get gradients.
(b) If FiLM is detached, its conditioning may be degenerate.
(c) `src/models/model.py:1982-2037` FiLM layers.
(d) Visualize FiLM modulation magnitude per input.

### A-3: What is the net throughput cost of sequence-mode PSR training given that the PSR head is detached from the backbone and still shows zero F1?

(a) PSR_SEQ_EVERY_N_BATCHES=4 consumes 25% of all batches for sequence mode. PSR head is detached from backbone (DETACH_PSR_FPN), F1=0.
(b) Sequence mode is paying 25% throughput cost for zero benefit.
(c) `src/config.py` PSR_SEQ_EVERY_N_BATCHES, train.log sequence=1 markers.
(d) Compare per-frame-only vs sequence-mode training loss curves.

### A-4: What is the transformer encoder's role when it processes T=1 sequences for 75% of batches?

(a) 3-layer transformer processes single-token sequences in per-frame mode.
(b) A 3-layer transformer on T=1 is wasteful.
(c) `src/models/model.py:1589` transformer warning.
(d) Remove transformer and measure impact on PSR.

### A-5: What is the FeatureBank's function when it is bypassed entirely or operates with gradient-detached historical entries?

(a) ACTIVITY_HEAD_SIMPLE=True and STAGED_TRAINING=False bypass the FeatureBank. DETACH_GRAD_ENTRIES_ONLY further isolates it.
(b) FeatureBank is dead code if all paths bypass it.
(c) `src/models/model.py` FeatureBank, `src/config.py` ACTIVITY_HEAD_SIMPLE.
(d) Remove FeatureBank and measure impact.

### A-6: What does the ACTIVITY_GRAD_BLEND_RATIO progression from 0.05 to 1.0 reveal about the c5_mod_blend gradient-scaling strategy?

(a) Five documented increases from 0.05 to 1.0 admit the blend mechanism failed and was gradually disabled.
(b) A reviewer will read this as: the activity head's gradient path was broken and you patched over it.
(c) `src/config.py` ACTIVITY_GRAD_BLEND_RATIO history.
(d) Train a fresh run without the blend and compare.

### A-7: What justifies the 3.1M-parameter PSR head when its gradient is isolated from the backbone, its F1 remains at zero, and its transformer processes T=1 sequences for 75% of batches?

(a) 95% of transformer capacity is in the PSR head, yet F1=0 and gradient-detached from backbone.
(b) Reviewers will question the parameter efficiency claim.
(c) `src/models/model.py` parameter counts.
(d) Compute parameter sensitivity (1M → 0.1M → 0.01M head).

---

## Section 6: Training Infrastructure — Deep Questions

### TI-1: Why does CUDNN_STATUS_EXECUTION_FAILED_CUDART strike at different steps each time?

(a) 4 crashes at steps 1290, 1137, 561, 909 — different each time. All on RTX 5060 Ti under batch_size=6. Stops crashing at batch_size=2.
(b) Reviewers will ask whether results are reproducible.
(c) `src/runs/full_multi_task_tma_tbank_benchmark/logs/train.log`.
(d) Bisect: batch_size=3, 4, 5 — find the threshold.

### TI-2: What is the true throughput tradeoff between batch_size=6 and batch_size=2 when crashes are factored in?

(a) batch_size=6 gets 0.4 batch/s but crashes every 1-3 hours. batch_size=2 gets 1.1 batch/s and runs stably. Effective samples/sec roughly equal (2.4 vs 2.2).
(b) Throughput claims should factor in stability, not just peak rate.
(c) `/tmp/train_ep24_smaller.log`, train.log history.
(d) Mean-time-to-crash (MTTC) measurement for each batch size.

### TI-3: Why does the PSR head produce zero loss with no gradient signal?

(a) PSR_DEBUG shows GELU fully saturated to zero after linear64. Numerical collapse.
(b) The PSR head may be unrecoverable without architecture change.
(c) train.log PSR_DEBUG lines.
(d) Replace GELU with ReLU and retrain.

### TI-4: How does gradient accumulation interact with CUDA kernel timeout on the RTX 5060 Ti?

(a) GRAD_ACCUM_STEPS=8 — 8 forward passes before 1 backward. Backward kernel may exceed CUDA TDR timeout.
(b) Reducing GRAD_ACCUM_STEPS may prevent crashes.
(c) `src/config.py` GRAD_ACCUM_STEPS=8.
(d) Test GRAD_ACCUM_STEPS=4 with batch_size=4.

### TI-5: What is the optimal GPU allocation strategy between RTX 5060 Ti (16 GB) and RTX 3060 (12 GB)?

(a) 5060 Ti at 78% idle VRAM while RTX 3060 runs a single-head ablation. DDP across both GPUs would accelerate ablations.
(b) Resource allocation is an underexploited opportunity.
(c) `nvidia-smi` snapshots during training.
(d) DDP setup experiment.

### TI-6: Why did the crashes begin at epoch 12 after 0 prior errors?

(a) Epochs 1-12 had 0 errors; epoch 12+ had 4 crashes. Progressive memory fragmentation, thermal accumulation, or RTX 3060 ablation starting mid-training are hypotheses.
(b) Reproducibility concern.
(c) `day1-checkpoint-done-and-next-steps.md` epoch 12 status.
(d) Run a clean training with explicit crash markers.

---

## Section 7: Eval Pipeline — Deep Questions

### EP-1: Class Index Alignment — Is There Really a 0-vs-1 Index Mismatch?

(a) eval_yolov8m.py asserts 0-indexed alignment, but D1 mAP=0.0004 suggests otherwise. PSR_MASK does `one_idx - 1`.
(b) If alignment is wrong, all detection metrics are invalid.
(c) `src/evaluation/eval_yolov8m.py:340-341`.
(d) Brute-force print of class distributions to confirm alignment.

### EP-2: RGB vs BGR Channel Order — Does Eval Match Training?

(a) Dataset loads RGB; eval converts to BGR. If training did NOT invert, eval feeds wrong-channel data.
(b) Silent detection degradation.
(c) `src/evaluation/eval_yolov8m.py:168, 330`.
(d) Unit test feeding known RGB through pipeline.

### EP-3: No Save-Interval for Crash Recovery in YOLOv8 Eval Scripts

(a) eval_yolov8m.py and eval_yolov8m_psr.py process full val set without intermediate saves. eval_activity_clip.py has save_interval=5000.
(b) Single point of failure on shared GPU infrastructure.
(c) `src/evaluation/eval_yolov8m.py:321-366`.
(d) Add `--save-every N` flag, default to 0 (no save).

### EP-4: Per-Component Threshold Selection — Validation Set Snooping?

(a) psr_optimal_thresholds.py sweeps 19 thresholds × 11 components on val. Reporting optimal F1 on the same val is overfitting.
(b) Reviewers will demand held-out test.
(c) `src/runs/rf_stages/checkpoints/psr_optimal_thr/optimal_thresholds.json`.
(d) Leave-one-recording-out CV.

### EP-5: Clip-Level Activity Eval — Does Majority-Vote Measure Anything?

(a) Majority-vote over 16 frames of per-frame MLP predictions, but MLP has zero temporal reasoning. Comparing to MViTv2-S (0.622) is apples-to-oranges.
(b) Reviewers will challenge the protocol.
(c) `src/evaluation/eval_activity_clip.py`.
(d) Test with shuffled-frame majority-vote to verify it gives same result.

---

## Section 8: SOTA Comparison — Deep Questions

### SOTA-1: Detection SOTA Claim — The D1 Audit Question

(a) Self-trained YOLOv8m achieves 0.995 mAP (beats WACV 0.838), but D1 full eval gives 0.0004. Is 0.0004 a class-mapping bug or a genuine protocol mismatch?
(b) Either claim has implications for paper narrative.
(c) `src/runs/rf_stages/checkpoints/d1_yolov8m/metrics.json`, `d1_yolov8m_v3/metrics.json`.
(d) Re-run D1 with explicit per-class histogram comparison.

### SOTA-2: STORM-PSR Paradigm Gap — Transition vs Per-Frame

(a) STORM F1=0.901 vs our F1=0.7499 — but different metrics (transition vs per-frame). D4 with same protocol as STORM gives F1=0.
(b) Mixing paradigms in a comparison table is invalid.
(c) `analyses/consult_2026_06_10/AAIML/industreal-sota-benchmarks.md`.
(d) Run STORM's metric on our predictions for direct comparison.

### SOTA-3: B3 Procedural Knowledge Confound

(a) B3 uses procedural knowledge (mask impossible transitions). Our decoder does not. Once our detection beats YOLOv8m (0.995), does the procedural-knowledge confound still apply?
(b) Fair comparison requires same constraints.
(c) `analyses/consult_2026_06_10/AAIML/reviewer-3-psr-paradigm-reconciliation.md`.
(d) Ablation: ours + procedural mask, ours without.

### SOTA-4: T3 MViTv2-S Baseline — Remapped Metric

(a) T3's 0.622 is on remapped classes. Our 0.028 is also remapped (hybrid mode). Is the comparison valid?
(b) Protocol verification needed.
(c) `src/runs/rf_stages/checkpoints/t3_full_eval.json`, `act_remap_75_to_69.json`.
(d) Run T3 on raw 75 classes for both protocols.

### SOTA-5: POS Paradox — Should We Report POS=0.968?

(a) Every piece of evidence confirms POS=0.968 is a fill-forward artifact. Should we drop POS from headline metrics?
(b) Reporting a metric artifact in the abstract is a reviewer attack.
(c) `analyses/consult_2026_06_10/AAIML/reviewer-3-psr-paradigm-reconciliation.md`.
(d) Null-model baseline: predict all-zeros, measure POS.

### SOTA-6: Per-Frame vs Clip-Level Activity — Defensible Comparison?

(a) Our 0.028 clip-level vs MViTv2-S 0.622 — apples-to-oranges. Any defensible frame-level comparison?
(b) Frame-level comparison may exist (T3 frame-level = ?).
(c) `analyses/consult_2026_06_10/AAIML/t3_mecanno_eval.json` (T3 frame-level = 0.04).
(d) Compute T3 frame-level on our eval protocol.

### SOTA-7: Ego-Pose Contribution — OpenFace/6DRepNet Distinction

(a) The "~15° SOTA" reference may be face-pose, not ego-pose. Survey legitimate ego-pose literature.
(b) Without distinction, readers conflate face and ego.
(c) `analyses/consult_2026_06_10/AAIML/reviewer-4-ego-pose-contribution.md`.
(d) Cite 3 legitimate ego-pose baselines.

### SOTA-8: The Efficiency/Accuracy Tradeoff — What Should the Paper Lead With?

(a) Beat SOTA on detection, OR systems efficiency story with competitive accuracy?
(b) The narrative direction determines which numbers go in the abstract.
(c) `analyses/consult_2026_06_10/AAIML/00_WIN_STRATEGY.md`.
(d) Survey AAIML 2026 accepted papers for narrative trends.

---

## Section 9: Paper Writing — Deep Questions

### PW-1: "Per-Frame Action Classification" vs "Activity Recognition" — Rename Consistently?

(a) The .tex file uses "Activity head" while the outline uses "per-frame action classification". Inconsistent naming across sections.
(b) Reviewers will be confused.
(c) `popw_aaiml2027.tex`, `22_REVISED_PAPER_OUTLINE.md`.
(d) grep -l "Activity recognition" across paper and rename.

### PW-2: How to Present Activity's 0.028 Top-1 Without Signaling Failure?

(a) The 0.028 number is essentially random on a 74-class problem. Either "different paradigm" or "deliberate trade-off" framing.
(b) Reviewers will see 0.028 as failure.
(c) `src/runs/rf_stages/checkpoints/SOTA_STATUS.md` activity row.
(d) Define the framing before writing §5.

### PW-3: A Language Framework for Numerical Claims

(a) When to use "beats SOTA," "competitive," "first baseline," "multi-task cost." Currently inconsistent (SOTA_STATUS uses all four).
(b) Inconsistent claim strength undermines credibility.
(c) `src/runs/rf_stages/checkpoints/SOTA_STATUS.md`.
(d) Draft a claim-strength rubric.

### PW-4: Ablation Table Design — One Combined vs Separate Figures?

(a) One combined ablation table vs separate per-head ablation figures.
(b) Reviewer attention is limited; one table is more likely to be read.
(c) `analyses/consult_2026_06_10/AAIML/06_TABLES_FIGURES.md`.
(d) Draft both and show to a reviewer.

### PW-5: Narrative Arc Tension — How Much Space for Deployment?

(a) The deployment section is 0.5–1.0 pages; the Best Paper formula gives it 20% weight. Tension between pathology focus and deployment focus.
(b) The paper's narrative direction must be coherent.
(c) `analyses/consult_2026_06_10/AAIML/04_BEST_PAPER_FORMULA.md`.
(d) Decide primary narrative thread.

### PW-6: PSR F1=0 Disclosure — Upfront vs Deferred?

(a) D4's F1=0 is the most damning number. Should it be in the abstract, the results table, or a deferred disclosure section?
(b) Reviewers will find it either way; placement signals confidence.
(c) `src/runs/rf_stages/checkpoints/d4_yolov8m_psr/metrics.json`.
(d) Test placements with a sample reviewer.

### PW-7: The Honesty Matrix — Narrative Device or Signal of Weakness?

(a) An explicit "honesty matrix" (what works, what doesn't) builds reviewer trust or signals weakness.
(b) Different reviewer preferences.
(c) `analyses/consult_2026_06_10/AAIML/24_REVIEWER_DEFENSE_V2.md`.
(d) Try a 0.5-page honesty matrix in §5.

---

## Section 10: Adversarial — Kill-Shot Questions

### AC-1: The Best-Checkpoint Selection Was Broken

(a) Epoch 11 was promoted to best.pth due to NaN-inflated metric. Every training decision before the fix used a broken evaluation signal.
(b) AAIML reviewers may demand re-running all evaluations from epoch 18+.
(c) `src/runs/rf_stages/checkpoints/best.pth` is now epoch 18 (post-fix).
(d) Audit which numbers used epoch 11 vs epoch 18.

### AC-2: Activity Clip-Level Top-1 = 0.028 vs SOTA 0.622

(a) 95.5% relative deficit. Effectively random on a 74-class problem. The paper claims five tasks but one head is non-functional.
(b) AAIML reviewers will reject a 5-task paper with 1 dead head.
(c) `src/runs/rf_stages/checkpoints/activity_clip_ep18/activity_clip.json`.
(d) Either retrain activity head or remove the activity claim.

### AC-3: The POS Paradox — Which PSR Number Is Real?

(a) Our F1=0.7499 vs D4 F1=0 with POS=0.999 in both. Same evaluation protocol. Which is real?
(b) Reviewers will spot the inconsistency.
(c) `SOTA_STATUS.md`, `d4_yolov8m_psr/metrics.json`.
(d) Run our PSR F1 on the D4 protocol for direct comparison.

### AC-4: Kendall Temperature Collapse — Did the PSR Head Actually Train?

(a) log_var_psr=-0.04, DEAD liveness, 569 zero-loss steps. F1 may be zero-shot backbone performance.
(b) The paper's central claim (multi-task learning) fails if one head didn't train.
(c) train.log PSR_DEBUG lines.
(d) Run KENDALL_FIXED_WEIGHTS=True ablation.

### AC-5: The Kendall Spiral Was Never Empirically Observed

(a) Pathology 2's empirical claim is unsupported. No training log shows the effect. Code parameters differ from paper description. Reframed internally as theoretical but paper says empirical.
(b) Internal contradictions are credibility killers.
(c) `analyses/consult_2026_06_10/AAIML/21_PATHOLOGY_CORRECTIONS.md`.
(d) Either reproduce the spiral or retract the claim.

### AC-6: All Numbers Will Change After the Simple Head Run

(a) R14 CRITICAL: every metric will differ after the simple head run. Detection has ranged 0.34–0.995, activity 18.3%–2.8%. No stable empirical foundation.
(b) The paper's central numbers may all change.
(c) `analyses/consult_2026_06_10/AAIML/11_NUMBERS_UPDATE.md`.
(d) Re-run all evals on the final simple-head checkpoint.

---
